summary: The post proposes a 'Leadership CI' framework—a set of self-assessment questions leaders should ask themselves before making decisions, analogous to how software uses continuous integration tests before merging code. Co-authored with Kathy Simpson, it argues that leaders need pre-established checks to avoid decisions driven by fear and pride, instead favoring growth, humility, and inclusion. The framework includes questions about ego, transparency, curiosity, manipulation, and whether you're empowering your team or creating an echo chamber.
key_points: Leaders need systematic checks before decisions, like CI tests for code
Fear and pride lead to devastating consequences; growth and humility lead to better outcomes
Key tests include: transparency, inclusion, curiosity, and whether your team feels safe disagreeing
The framework should evolve over time as new situations arise
Self-serving decisions fail the test; morally good decisions with long-term impact pass
money_quotes: There are devastating consequences to taking actions based on fear and pride; we hope this set of questions enables taking action based on growth, humility, inclusion, and soulful reflection.

As leaders, before acting or making decisions it would be nice to have a set of tests and checks, established ahead of time, to make sure we are in the right headspace to think, behave and make decisions that are in the best interest of everyone and our company.
Pass: I'm asking questions that make me uncomfortable, and I'm comfortable being wrong. Fail: I want my way.

Pass: They freely and continually come to me with answers and information that they know I will not like. Fail: They go to each other or people outside the team with the information, and telling me what they think I want to hear.
Pass: I am doing the minority of the speaking and people are disagreeing with my opinions. Fail: I am being quoted back to myself.
key_insight: Just as software engineers use CI to catch bugs before they ship, leaders should use a personal checklist of introspective questions to catch ego-driven or fear-based decisions before they cause harm.
